what: Pairwise Conditional Rasch Measurement Analysis and Diagnostics
title: Pairwise Conditional Rasch Measurement Analysis and Diagnostics
text: Documentation: https://drjoshmcgrane.github.io/rmt/ pairwise conditional Rasch measurement engine R, built entirely published measurement theory. Item estimation pairwise conditional maximum likelihood (Andrich & Luo 2003; Zwinderman 1995); person locations use Warm’s weighted likelihood; diagnostic suite follows conventions set Andrich & Marais (2019) — log--mean-square fit residual apportioned degrees freedom, automatically sized class intervals, item-trait chi-square per-interval detail table, ANOVA item-fit F — covers reliability, targeting, dimensionality (magnitude estimation), local dependence (magnitude estimation), guessing (tailored analysis), DIF multiple person factors; modern Shiny interface exposes lot one-click export every table plot.

title: Pairwise Conditional Rasch Measurement Analysis and Diagnostics
text: responses persons--items matrix, data frame also carries ID column person factors. Item scores integers starting 0; missing data allowed (estimation pairwise, person measures use person’s observed items). Empty categories collapsed constant items dropped, notes recorded fit.
code: library(rmt)  # data frame with an ID column, item columns (names carry through), and # person factors for DIF fit <- rasch(responses, model = "PCM",              id = "person_id", factors = c("gender", "site"))  summary(fit)         # full test-of-fit report fit$items            # location + SE, fit residual, infit/outfit, chi-square fit$person           # WLE + SE per person, with ID, factors, raw score, person fit fit$thresholds       # every threshold with its standard error score_table(fit)     # raw score to measure conversion fit$psi; fit$psi_noext; fit$alpha   # PSI (with/without extremes), Cronbach's alpha  plot_icc(fit, "Q05")                  # ICC with observed class-interval means plot_icc(fit, "Q05", group = "gender")  # ICC by group: the graphical DIF display plot_ccc(fit, "Q05")                  # category probability curves plot_threshold_prob(fit, "Q05")       # threshold probability ogives plot_pimap(fit)                       # person-item threshold distribution plot_threshold_map(fit)               # all thresholds by item plot_tcc(fit); plot_tif(fit)          # test characteristic and information curves plot_item_map(fit); plot_person_fit(fit) plot_resid_cor(fit); plot_pca(fit)  chisq_detail(fit, "Q05")   # per-class-interval chi-square detail table score_table(fit, method = "mle", extremes = "extrapolated")                            # complete-data estimates with geometric                            # extreme-score extrapolation, frequencies, cum % ctt_table(fit)             # traditional statistics: facility, item-total r,                            # discrimination index, alpha, classical SEM lr_test(fit)               # PCM vs rating parameterisation: the raw                            # composite chi-square + Kent-calibrated version  dimensionality_test(fit)   # residual-PCA contrast + person t-test + paired t dimensionality_magnitude(fit, list(setA, setB))                            # Andrich (2016): c, latent subscale correlation                            # rho = 1/(1+c^2), common-variance proportion A residual_correlations(fit) # local dependence dependence_magnitude(fit, dependent = "Q05", independent = "Q04")                            # Andrich & Kreiner d in logits, with SE and test dif_anova(fit)             # uniform and non-uniform DIF per factor: the full                            # two-way table (group, class interval, interaction)                            # with partial eta-squared effect sizes; BH, Holm,                            # or Bonferroni adjustment across items dif_anova_factorial(fit, sizes = TRUE)                            # all factors jointly: x-way factorial ANOVA per                            # item with interactions, interaction-precedence,                            # Tukey HSD post-hocs on significant group terms                            # (interaction cells included), and DIF magnitudes                            # in logits for every significant term dif_size(fit, "Q05", by = "gender")                            # DIF magnitude on the measurement scale: resolved                            # locations per level (or interaction cell), all                            # pairwise differences with Holm familywise                            # adjustment and a practical-significance flag                            # (default 0.5 logits) tailored_analysis(fit, chance = 0.25)                            # four-step guessing procedure (dichotomous MC)  # local dependence remedy: combine dependent items into a subtest and refit, # then screen the super-items' spread against the binomial bound fit2 <- combine_items(fit, list(c("Q04", "Q05"))) spread_test(fit2)          # spread below the LUB indicates dependence  # DIF remedy: split the offending item by the factor and refit; each group # receives its own location and the distance between them is the DIF size fit3 <- split_items(fit, "Q05", by = "gender")  # equating: anchor nominated thresholds (or item means, with k = NA) at # fixed values; everything else is estimated on the anchored scale and # person measures become comparable across separately analysed datasets fit_eq <- rasch(responses, anchors = data.frame(item = c("Q01", "Q10"),                                                 k = c(1, NA), tau = c(-1.2, 0.9)))  # equating check: compare two calibrations through their common items eq <- equate_tests(fit, fit_eq)   # or a bank: data.frame(item, location, se) eq$table                          # t-tests against the shifted identity line plot_equate(fit, fit_eq)  # many-facet Rasch model for rated, long-format data (one row per response) mf <- rasch_mfrm(ratings, person = "person", item = "criterion",                  score = "score", facets = "rater") mf$facet_effects$rater    # severities with SEs and pooled fit per rater plot_facets(mf)           # severity caterpillar plot  # interactive facet mode: lets a rater be severe on particular items mfi <- rasch_mfrm(ratings, person = "person", item = "criterion",                   score = "score", facets = "rater", interaction = "rater") mfi$interaction_effects   # item-by-rater gamma with SEs  # multiple choice: score raw responses against a key, keep them for # distractor analysis (rest-measure based, with miskey flagging) mc <- rasch(responses_raw, key = c(Q1 = "A", Q2 = "C", Q3 = "B")) distractor_analysis(mc)    # per option: n, proportion, mean location, point-biserial plot_distractors(mc, "Q2") # option curves over class intervals  # double keying: several correct options, all scoring 1 mc2 <- rasch(responses_raw, key = c(Q1 = "A/C", Q2 = "C", Q3 = "B"))  # polytomous option scoring (Andrich & Styles 2011): informative # distractors receive partial credit and the item is fitted as polytomous; # distractor_rescore() proposes the scoring from the rest-measure evidence prop <- distractor_rescore(mc) prop$option_scores         # item, option, score - review, edit, refit: mc3 <- rasch(responses_raw, key = prop$option_scores)  # extended frame of reference model: the unit of the scale differs across # item-set by person-group frames (rho = alpha_set x phi_group) ef <- rasch_efrm(responses, item_sets = list(numeracy = num_items,                                              literacy = lit_items),                  groups = "year_group") ef$phi_table; ef$alpha_table   # group and set units with SEs ef$frames                      # one row per frame: unit, origin, pooled fit plot_frames(ef)                # unit caterpillar plot_icc_frames(ef, "Q07")     # fanned ICCs: parallel within, fanned across  # paired comparisons: the Bradley-Terry-Luce model, the conditional # (person-free) form of the dichotomous Rasch model, estimated by the same # conventions -- sum-zero identification, sandwich SEs (judge-clustered), # fit residuals for objects and judges, pairwise goodness of fit bt <- btl(comparisons, object_a = "left", object_b = "right",           winner = "preferred", judge = "judge") bt$objects; bt$judges     # locations + fit; erratic judges flagged plot_btl(bt)              # object caterpillar  # compare model fits: same-data fits get the pairwise conditional # log-likelihood difference (descriptive); all fits get calibration-free # fit descriptors (chi-square/df, fit residual SDs, PSI, alpha) compare_fits(PCM = fit, RSM = rasch(responses, model = "RSM"))  save_outputs(fit, "results/")   # every table (CSV), every plot (PNG + PDF), summary.txt

text: data data frame one comparison per row. object_a, object_b Names columns holding two objects compared. winner Name column holding winner row: value must equal row's object_a object_b entry; "tie" "draw" marks tie; anything else (including blanks) treated missing dropped note. Ignored response given. response Optional name column holding graded preference object_a object_b – ordered factor (worst best object_a) integer scores 0..m. Fits adjacent-categories ordinal extension BTL (Tutz 1986; Agresti 1992): partial-credit structure difference locations thresholds constrained symmetric, tau_k = -tau_(m+1-k), model invariant presentation order. Two categories reproduce BTL exactly; three give Davidson (1970) ties model. margin Optional name column holding extent win ("little", "much", ...), ordered factor increasing values; combined winner assembles graded response without orientation bookkeeping ("B much" means thing whichever column B sits ). Winner values matching neither object ties form middle category. judge Optional name judge column; enables judge fit table clusters sandwich standard errors judge. count Optional name column replication counts (row standing several identical comparisons). order Optional name column giving judge's judgment sequence (timestamps ranks; requires judge). Adds within-judge dependence analysis: exposure effect (advantage, logits, object judge seen one ) carry-effect (pull judge's earlier verdicts object – response dependence sense Marais Andrich 2008), estimated jointly locations reported dependence. Incompatible ties = "half". ties treat ties dichotomous analysis: "drop" (default, removed note), "half" (half win way, common pragmatic device – flagged notes halves independent Bernoulli trials), "error". graded responses, code ties middle category instead. thresholds "free" (default) estimates every symmetric threshold parameter; "pc" pools spread (linear) principal component – symmetric case principal-component threshold structure, whose even skewness component structurally zero – thinly used categories borrow strength every response. modes report component decomposition. maxit, tol Newton-Raphson iteration cap convergence tolerance.

title: Fit the Bradley-Terry-Luce model to paired comparisons — btl
code: set.seed(1) beta <- c(A = -1, B = -0.3, C = 0.4, D = 0.9) pairs <- t(combn(names(beta), 2)) d <- data.frame(a = rep(pairs[, 1], each = 30),                 b = rep(pairs[, 2], each = 30)) p <- plogis(beta[d$a] - beta[d$b]) d$win <- ifelse(runif(nrow(d)) < p, d$a, d$b) btl(d, object_a = "a", object_b = "b", winner = "win") #> Bradley-Terry-Luce analysis: 4 objects, 180 comparisons #> Conditional ML: converged in 6 iterations; sandwich SEs #> Object separation index 0.963; pairwise chi-square 1.07 on 3 df, p = 0.783 #>  object location    se comparisons wins fit_resid #>       A   -1.238 0.214          90   16    -0.111 #>       B   -0.354 0.186          90   36     0.526 #>       C    0.448 0.180          90   56    -0.056 #>       D    1.144 0.209          90   72     0.037

code: set.seed(1) beta <- c(A = -1, B = -0.3, C = 0.4, D = 0.9) pr <- t(combn(names(beta), 2)) d <- data.frame(a = rep(pr[, 1], each = 60), b = rep(pr[, 2], each = 60),                 judge = sample(sprintf("J%02d", 1:12), 360, TRUE)) shift <- ifelse(d$judge %in% sprintf("J%02d", 1:6) & d$a == "C", 0.9,          ifelse(d$judge %in% sprintf("J%02d", 1:6) & d$b == "C", -0.9, 0)) p <- plogis(beta[d$a] - beta[d$b] + shift) d$win <- ifelse(runif(nrow(d)) < p, d$a, d$b) f <- btl(d, "a", "b", winner = "win", judge = "judge") grp <- setNames(rep(c("g1", "g2"), each = 6), sprintf("J%02d", 1:12)) btl_dif(f, groups = grp, objects = "C") #> DIF for paired comparisons: 1 object(s) by judge group #> Residual ANOVA (uniform = group; non-uniform = group x opponent band; BH across objects) #>  object   n F_uniform p_uniform_adj uniform_DIF F_nonuniform p_nonuniform_adj #>       C 180    14.495       < 0.001           *        0.081            0.922 #>  nonuniform_DIF #>                 #>  #> Resolved locations (logits; holm over 1 comparison(s); practical 0.50) #>  object level_a level_b difference    se     z   p_adj significant practical #>       C      g1      g2      1.404 0.271 5.186 < 0.001           *         *

title: Estimate the magnitude of response dependence between two items — dependence_magnitude
text: Quantifies strongly dependent item's response follows independent item's response, logits, resolution method Andrich Kreiner (2010; polytomous generalisation Andrich, Humphry Marais 2012), resolution dependent item. dependent item resolved one item per category independent item (carrying responses persons gave category), original items removed, model refitted. dependence magnitude \(d\), threshold \(k\) resolved item category \(x_i\) shifted \(-d\) \(k \le x_i\) \(+d\) otherwise, threshold yields \(\hat d_k = (\hat\delta_{ji(k)}(x_i = k-1) - \hat\delta_{ji(k)}(x_i = k))/2\) \(\hat d\) mean (eq. 24.7 Andrich Marais 2019). resolved items answered disjoint persons, estimates independent, standard error pools threshold variances: \(\hat\sigma^2_k = (\hat\sigma^2_{(k)(k-1)} + \hat\sigma^2_{(k)(k)})/4\), \(V[\hat d] = \bar{\hat\sigma^2_k}/m\) (eqs. 24.9-24.11).

Missing values allowed. model Either "PCM" (partial credit) "RSM" (rating scale). id Optional name ID column data, vector IDs; carried person estimates. factors Optional character vector person-factor column names data (DIF analysis), data frame factors. items Optional character vector naming item columns; default every column named id factors. n_groups Number class intervals item-trait chi-square ANOVA item fit. default NULL applies rule Andrich Marais (2019, ch. 15): many intervals least 50 non-extreme persons sample allows, 10, least 2. resolved value stored fit$n_groups. adjust_N Optional reference sample size; supplied, item-trait chi-squares rescaled size (sample-size adjustment sensitivity chi-square large samples). anchors Optional anchor table equating: data frame columns item, k, tau fixing nominated thresholds known values; see pcml. anchors place scale origin comes anchors, person measures directly comparable across separately analysed datasets. na_codes Values read missing. Defaults -1, conventional missing-response code; negative score also treated missing, since valid category scores start zero. key Optional multiple-choice scoring key, three forms. (1) named vector data frame columns item key naming item's correct option: scored 0/1 (case-insensitive trimming; blanks become missing). (2) Double keying: several correct options separated "/" (example "/C"), scoring 1. (3) Polytomous option scoring (Andrich Styles 2011): data frame columns item, option, score assigning integer score every credited option (unlisted options score 0), informative distractors receive partial credit item fitted polytomous; see distractor_rescore evidence-based proposal. Every item--facet combination becomes virtual item whose thresholds item's thresholds shifted facet severities, whole structure estimated one pass pairwise conditional likelihood, person parameter cancels. Facet severities reported standard errors pooled fit statistics; returned object also full rasch fit virtual-item level, every diagnostic table plot package applies .

code: rasch_mfrm(   data,   person,   item = NULL,   score = NULL,   facets,   items = NULL,   n_groups = NULL,   adjust_N = NA,   na_codes = -1,   interaction = NULL,   maxit = 60,   tol = 1e-08 )

title: Fit a many-facet Rasch model — rasch_mfrm
text: data Long-format data frame. person Name person identifier column. item Name item column. score Name integer score column (categories 0; gaps collapsed per item note). facets Character vector naming one facet columns (example rater column). items Optional character vector item score columns data wide format: one row per person--facet combination (example one row per script per rater) one column per item criterion. long form (item + score) remains available data facet varies within items. n_groups Number class intervals item-trait chi-square; NULL (default) applies class-interval rule Andrich Marais (2019, ch. 15) (least 50 non-extreme persons per interval, 10 intervals, least 2). adjust_N Optional reference sample size chi-square. na_codes Score values read missing (default -1); negative score also treated missing. interaction Optional name one facet interact items (interactive facet mode). Adds item--facet terms gamma[item, level] double sum--zero constraints top additive severities, level may less severe particular items; estimates returned interaction_effects. interactive model remains Rasch class (discriminations equal one parameters additive), significant interaction qualifies specific objectivity practice: comparisons interacting facet's levels become item-dependent, substantive finding. maxit, tol Newton-Raphson iteration cap convergence tolerance.

title: Fit a many-facet Rasch model — rasch_mfrm
text: object classes "rasch_mfrm" "rasch".   addition every component rasch fit (computed   virtual items), carries facet_effects (per facet: level,   severity, standard error, observation count, pooled fit),   item_effects (underlying item locations pooled fit),   item_thresholds (structural delta_ik standard   errors), facet_spec. Two fit residuals reported per   facet level per underlying item. fit_resid   facet-margin statistic published three-facet fit tables   (Andrich Marais 2019, ch. 26 app. C), mean   constituent virtual items'   fit residuals; weighs virtual item equally, erratic level   shows average per-item misfit. fit_resid_pooled   log--mean-square statistic summed margin's   observed cells non-extreme persons, degrees freedom   df_fit; weighs response equally   powerful statistic misfit spread evenly level's   cells.

title: Fit a many-facet Rasch model — rasch_mfrm
code: set.seed(1) simP <- function(th, tau) { x <- 0:length(tau); p <- exp(x * th - c(0, cumsum(tau))); p / sum(p) } persons <- sprintf("P%03d", 1:120); raters <- paste0("R", 1:4) th <- setNames(rnorm(120, 0, 1.3), persons) rho <- setNames(c(-0.6, -0.2, 0.2, 0.6), raters) tau <- list(A = c(-1, 1), B = c(-0.5, 1.2), C = c(-1.2, 0.4)) d <- expand.grid(person = persons, item = names(tau), rater = raters,                  stringsAsFactors = FALSE) d$score <- mapply(function(p, i, r)   sample(0:2, 1, prob = simP(th[p], tau[[i]] + rho[r])), d$person, d$item, d$rater) fit <- rasch_mfrm(d, person = "person", item = "item", score = "score",                   facets = "rater") fit$facet_effects$rater #>    level   severity         se   n  infit_ms outfit_ms    fit_resid #> R1    R1 -0.6741794 0.08866656 354 1.0748808 1.0555789  0.352198069 #> R2    R2 -0.1984195 0.07726631 354 1.0559605 1.0389336  0.337676916 #> R3    R3  0.1850839 0.07784122 354 1.0009494 0.9972743 -0.007416974 #> R4    R4  0.6875150 0.07293532 354 0.9666351 0.9568306 -0.336987303 #>    fit_resid_pooled df_fit #> R1      0.736766065  322.5 #> R2      0.548246161  322.5 #> R3      0.008326603  322.5 #> R4     -0.508911000  322.5

title: Tailored analysis for guessing — tailored_analysis
text: Runs four-step tailored procedure Andrich, Marais Humphry (2012) dichotomous analysis. Step 1 supplied fit. Step 2 (tailored) sets missing every observed response whose modelled probability success, step-1 person item estimates, chance, re-estimates items persons. Step 3 (origin-equated) re-analyses original data mean location anchor items fixed tailored values average anchoring, two calibrations share origin. Step 4 (-anchored) fixes every item tailored difficulty re-estimates persons original data. Guessing indicated difficult items estimated harder tailored analysis origin-equated one; comparison table plot_equate two calibrations show directly.

rmt 1.0.0
text: First stable release. package delivers complete Rasch Measurement Theory workflow built entirely published measurement theory, pairwise conditional estimation core Shiny interface exposes every analysis reproducing R code attached every output.

title: rmt 1.0.0
text: rasch(): dichotomous polytomous (partial credit rating scale) Rasch models pairwise conditional maximum likelihood (Andrich & Luo 2003; Zwinderman 1995), Godambe sandwich standard errors, sum-zero identification, Warm (1989) weighted likelihood person estimation, principal-component threshold parameterisation estimation option. rasch_mfrm(): many-facet models additive facet severities item--facet interactions, accepting wide (one column per item) long data. rasch_efrm(): extended frame--reference model (Humphry) frame units estimated within-frame pairwise conditional likelihood. btl(): paired comparisons conditional form dichotomous Rasch model (Bradley & Terry 1952; Andrich 1978), including adjacent-categories graded extension (Tutz 1986; Agresti 1992) symmetric thresholds – Davidson (1970) ties model three-category case – winner-plus-margin data entry, judge-clustered errors, within-judge exposure carry-effects estimated jointly locations.

title: rmt 1.0.0
text: test--fit suite follows Andrich & Marais (2019): log--mean-square fit residuals apportioned degrees freedom, item-trait chi-square automatically sized, tie-preserving class intervals per-interval detail, ANOVA item fit, person fit. Unidimensionality: residual principal components parallel analysis, Smith (2002) subset t-test, magnitude estimation. Local dependence: Yen’s (1984) Q3 Christensen, Makransky & Horton (2017) flagging convention, response-dependence magnitude (Andrich & Kreiner), subtest formation, spread test. Guessing: tailored analysis origin-equated comparison calibrations. DIF: two-way residual ANOVA per factor, joint factorial analysis compact uniform/non-uniform summary, resolved DIF magnitudes logits familywise control practical-significance criterion, planned contrasts derived factor structure (repeated-measures support via person-level residual scores), class intervals sized cells analysis actually uses. paired comparisons, btl_dif() tests object--judge-group interaction two routes. Equating invariance: common-item comparison drift flags, calibration anchoring.

title: rmt 1.0.0
text: complete base-graphics plot suite: expected value curves observed class-interval means, category threshold probability curves, person-item threshold distribution, Wright maps, kidmaps (Wright, Mead & Ludlow 1980), object characteristic curves paired comparisons, threshold item maps, test characteristic information curves, residual displays, Guttman scalograms – adjustable class intervals scale ranges, batch export multi-page PDF ZIP publication resolution. fit_summary_table() targeting_table() return headline statistics tidy tables; save_outputs() writes every table plot disk; report_html() produces single-file HTML report. run_app() launches Shiny interface: model-adaptive navigation, reproducing R code beneath every output, one-click export.
